items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: Students assume 'three sections' means three different ones. Ternary has only TWO distinct ideas, but A returns - it's the return that makes it ternary, not the section count alone.

items.q: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
items.why: Students count fifth as 'five steps' but the name refers to letter NAMES (1-2-3-4-5), not semitones. C to G spans 5 letter names but 7 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G).

items.q: What is a tone row in serial music?
items.correct: An ordered series of all 12 chromatic pitches, used as the basis of a piece.
items.misconception: A row of notes played together as a chord.
items.random_wrong: Any sequence of notes a composer chooses.
items.why: Students miss the SPECIFIC structure. Schoenberg's 12-tone technique uses ALL 12 pitches in a fixed order before any repeats, generating melodies and harmonies from that row.

items.q: In sonata form, in what key does the second subject normally appear in the exposition of a piece in a major key?
items.why: Correct answer: Dominant. “Tonic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which composer is credited with significantly expanding the coda in sonata-form movements?
items.why: It's Beethoven. “Mozart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main sections does classical sonata form contain (exposition, development, recapitulation)?
items.why: The real figure is 3 sections. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
